_meta.purpose: Public content manifest for the Bassam Anouti site and the future bilingual (EN/FR) site and digital identity. This file is deployed and publicly downloadable; it contains approved, public-ready values only.
_meta.internal_companion: internal-research.json — internal research, provenance, caveats and unverified leads; NOT deployed and NOT public.

artist.name: Bassam Anouti
artist.tagline: At the intersection of classical visual culture and contemporary digital practice.
bio: Bassam Anouti is a multidisciplinary artist, art historian, educator and digital image-maker whose work is shaped by a broad academic and creative formation across art history, French literature, clinical psychology, fine arts, fashion design, jewelry design and graphic design.

His practice brings together research, image-making and visual storytelling. Working across photography, digital painting, compositing and mixed media, Anouti develops images that are both constructed and narrative. His work is grounded in historical, anthropological, literary and mythological research, without being limited to illustration. Each image is conceived as a visual situation in which characters, symbols and cultural references enter into tension.

Narration plays a central role in his artistic language. The figures and scenes he constructs establish a dialogue with the viewer, opening a space of interpretation rather than delivering a fixed meaning. His images often operate through confrontation, ambiguity and psychological intensity, allowing the spectator to participate in the story being staged.

Alongside his artistic practice, Anouti has developed a strong pedagogical practice in the visual arts, with a focus on contemporary image-making, digital tools and creative methodologies. He has also worked with UNDP as a trainer and mentor, contributing to programs dedicated to visual development and digital creation.

Anouti’s work stands at the intersection of classical visual culture and contemporary digital practice. It reflects a sustained interest in collective memory, symbolic systems, cultural hybridization and the narrative power of images.

philosophy.title: Order Against Chaos
paragraphs: Having come of age in the chaos of the Lebanese Civil War, Anouti witnessed disorder firsthand. His response has been to make work that is cérébrale, ordonnée et réfléchie — cerebral, ordered, and reflective — a deliberate counter-narrative to the socio-political and human disorder he lived through.

Drawing on principles of psychology, his art-making functions as an intellectual exercise: by imposing meticulous structure, symmetry, and thought, he symbolically mends the fractures of a broken world. His compositions are concept-driven and highly deliberate, the inverse of the randomness of a war-torn reality.

His art stages a dialogue between Eastern and Western iconography — weaving Babylonian and Levantine mythologies through European technique, juxtaposing the ancient with the modern. Anouti assumes an audience with artistic and literary appreciation, and a historical understanding of Eastern and Western cultures, as well as their symbolism.

navigation.label: Work
navigation.note: Maps to the existing /series/ route; contains the two series below.

navigation_note: Public navigation is Work · About · Contact, plus a future EN/FR language toggle. 'Work' is the Series route and contains Iconic and Bestiaire Mythique.

media.note: Every image `src` in this manifest is relative to multipage/ (i.e. multipage/media/cropped/…) and is a CROPPED FRAGMENT — never a complete artwork. These cropped fragments are the only approved public artwork images for the series pages. Do NOT use the full artworks under the root images/ directory, and do NOT extract images from the PDFs, for series content. The single, deliberate exception is the homepage hero (see homepage_hero_exception).
media.homepage_hero_exception: The homepage hero is one full artwork (Semiramis and Ninyas) shown only on the home page. It lives OUTSIDE this crop manifest: it is referenced directly by the homepage (a full-artwork file under the repository root images/ directory, via the Codex-owned index.html), not from media/cropped/. It is a deliberate homepage-only exception and does NOT change the cropped-fragments-only policy for the series pages. It is intentionally not listed among the crops here.

series.title: Iconic
series.subtitle: Iconic I — a fresco of transgression
series.statement: Iconic I is the first chapter of a large fresco featuring legendary, folkloric, mythical and historical characters who have left a mark on history and on the collective imagination. The themes are woven around unhealthy passions, transgressed taboos, rape, murder, incest, regicide, matricide, infanticide, fratricide and parricide. The artist tries to capture the magnificence of monstrosity — a XXth–XXIst century trend in which transgressive figures, beyond good and evil in the nietzschean sense, are glamorized, looked upon, and put on a pedestal. The result of in-depth research in mythology, history, politics, folklore, literature and anthropology, the series was built from photographs taken in Lebanon and France, then transformed through digital painting and compositing, printed as a single artwork using ultraviolet light before being reworked using traditional fine-art techniques.

works.title: Semiramis and Ninyas
works.summary: Queen of Assyria, builder of cities and empires — wife, widow and incestuous mother — whose rise, by valour in some accounts and by treachery in others, ends with her seizing the throne.

images.alt: Ninyas (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti
Ninyas, 'Mama's Boy' (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Agrippina and Nero
works.summary: Agrippina turns her sexual aggression toward her own son Nero in a bid to keep power; Nero has her murdered, and she reportedly bids her killer strike the womb that bore him.

images.alt: Agrippina and Nero (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Messalina and Britannicus
works.summary: Claudius' young wife Messalina, cast by the sources as a scheming nymphomaniac, and her son Britannicus — the heir later poisoned on Nero's orders.

images.alt: Messalina and the child Britannicus (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Constantine I and Crispus
works.summary: The first Roman emperor to profess Christianity and his son Crispus, executed by his father on a charge later found to be false.

works.title: Rodrigo and Lucrezia Borgia
works.summary: Pope Alexander VI (Rodrigo Borgia) and his daughter Lucrezia, whose family name became a byword for corruption, poison and intrigue in the Renaissance papacy.

images.alt: Rodrigo (as Pope Alexander VI) and Lucrezia Borgia (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Al-Khayzuran
works.summary: A slave who became wife of Caliph Al-Mahdi and mother of two caliphs, ruling in all but name — and rumoured to have had her own son suffocated when he crossed her.

images.alt: Al-Khayzuran (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Sitt al Mulk and Al Hakim
works.summary: The Fatimid princess who took the reins of government from her brother Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah and is said to have plotted his death.

images.alt: Sitt al Mulk with the severed head of Al Hakim (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti
Sitt al Mulk (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Shajara al-Durr and Izz ad-Din Aybak
works.summary: The slave who became Sultana of Egypt, ran the state through war and, when her husband Aybak sought to replace her, plotted his murder — and was beaten to death in turn.

images.alt: Shajara al-Durr (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti
Izz ad-Din Aybak (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Seth and Horus
works.summary: From the Contendings of Horus and Seth: the fratricidal god Seth and his nephew Horus contend for the throne of Egypt through violence and seduction.

images.alt: Seth and Horus (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti
Seth and Horus, costume detail — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Tantalus and Pelops
works.summary: Tantalus, testing the gods' omniscience, serves them his own son Pelops at a feast; the boy is restored to life and Tantalus is condemned to eternal torment.

images.alt: Tantalus and the child Pelops (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Procne and Itys
works.summary: Avenging her sister Philomela, Procne kills her son Itys and serves him to his father Tereus.

works.title: Phaedra and Hippolytus
works.summary: Struck by Aphrodite's curse, Phaedra falls for her stepson Hippolytus; rejected, she accuses him falsely and he is destroyed by his father's curse.

images.alt: Phaedra and Hippolytus (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Jocasta and Oedipus
works.summary: Jocasta and the son she abandoned, Oedipus, who unknowingly kills his father and marries his mother — ending in her suicide and his self-blinding.

images.alt: Jocasta and the child Oedipus (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Baba Yaga
works.medium: UV digital print and mixed media
works.summary: The witch of Slavic folklore who lives by her own rules in a forest hut — helper, captor and devourer alike — increasingly read as a figure of feminine power and the trickster archetype.

images.alt: Baba Yaga (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Kuchisake-onna
works.summary: The 'slit-mouthed woman' of Japanese folklore, a vengeful spirit mutilated by a jealous husband who stalks passers-by with her scissors.

images.alt: Kuchisake-onna (detail) — from the Iconic series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: La Llorona
works.summary: The 'Weeping Woman' of Mexico and the Southwest who drowned her children and, denied the afterlife, wanders the rivers forever searching for them.

series.title: Bestiaire Mythique
series.statement: Bestiaire Mythique stages mythical creatures — the Adams and Eves of a new cosmogony. Through research in anthropology, literature and mythology, Anouti invents new races of hybrid beings, half-human and half-animal, borrowing from and reinventing monsters and creatures of Greek, Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Nordic and Chinese mythologies so that they people a contemporary collective imaginary whose roots are anchored in the past of civilizations. Each work passes through anthropological, historical and mythological research, then studio photography, digital painting and compositing; it is printed on a chosen support and sometimes reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf, remaining a unique, single edition.

works.title: Kinnara
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A half-human, half-bird hybrid drawn from Indian mythology.

images.alt: Kinnara — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Alconost
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A half-human, half-bird hybrid drawn from Russian mythology.

images.alt: Alconost — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Anggitay I
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A centaur-like hybrid drawn from Philippine mythology.

images.alt: Anggitay I — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Centaur I
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A centaur hybrid drawn from Greek mythology.

images.alt: Centaur I — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Kudan I
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A bovine-human hybrid drawn from Japanese folklore.

images.alt: Kudan I — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Kudan II
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A bovine-human hybrid drawn from Japanese folklore.

images.alt: Kudan II — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Naga
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A serpent-human hybrid drawn from Hindu (Indian) mythology.

images.alt: Naga — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Shahmaran
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A serpent-woman hybrid drawn from Turkish and Persian folklore.

images.alt: Shahmaran — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Praying Mantis I
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A human–praying-mantis hybrid.

images.alt: Praying Mantis I — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Praying Mantis II
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A human–praying-mantis hybrid.

images.alt: Praying Mantis II — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Rd 03
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A portrait bust from the Hybrides group — a hybrid, primordial divinity transcending cultures and time.

images.alt: Hybride portrait (Rd 03) — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

works.title: Rd 04
works.medium: Digital painting and mixed media; unique print (occasionally reworked with paint, ink or gold leaf)
works.summary: A portrait bust from the Hybrides group — a hybrid, primordial divinity transcending cultures and time.

images.alt: Hybride portrait (Rd 04) — from the Bestiaire Mythique series by Bassam Anouti

exhibitions_note: No exhibition is published publicly, and 'Exhibitions' is not part of the public navigation. Iconic and Bestiaire Mythique are series (see 'series'), not exhibitions.
